terms.definition: Difficulty swallowing food, fluids or saliva, often due to neurological or structural causes.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was referred to SLT for assessment of dysphagia following a left MCA stroke.
terms.contextNotes: Always document consistency tolerated and any signs of aspiration.

terms.definition: Swallowing difficulty arising from the mouth or pharynx, typically with delayed or weak swallow trigger.
terms.exampleSentence: Bedside assessment indicated oropharyngeal dysphagia with delayed pharyngeal trigger.
terms.contextNotes: Most common dysphagia type seen by SLT in acute stroke care.

terms.definition: Swallowing difficulty arising from the oesophagus, often described as food sticking after swallowing.
terms.exampleSentence: Patient reports oesophageal dysphagia and has been referred for barium swallow.
terms.contextNotes: Usually managed by gastroenterology rather than SLT alone.

terms.definition: The entry of food, fluid or saliva below the level of the vocal folds into the airway.
terms.exampleSentence: Coughing on thin fluids was observed, suggesting aspiration during the swallow.
terms.contextNotes: Document timing: before, during or after the swallow.

terms.definition: Aspiration of material into the airway without any outward signs such as coughing or throat clearing.
terms.exampleSentence: Videofluoroscopy confirmed silent aspiration of thin fluids despite no clinical cough.
terms.contextNotes: Cannot be reliably detected at the bedside; instrumental assessment needed.

terms.definition: The entry of material into the laryngeal vestibule but remaining above the vocal folds.
terms.exampleSentence: VFSS showed penetration to the level of the vocal folds without aspiration.
terms.contextNotes: Scored using the Penetration-Aspiration Scale (PAS, 1-8).

terms.definition: Food or fluid remaining in the oral cavity, valleculae or pyriform sinuses after the swallow.
terms.exampleSentence: Significant vallecular residue was noted post-swallow on instrumental assessment.
terms.contextNotes: Residue increases the risk of post-swallow aspiration.

terms.definition: The anticipatory stage of swallowing involving sight, smell and approach of food to the mouth.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient showed reduced engagement during the pre-oral phase, affecting intake.
terms.contextNotes: Often impaired in dementia or visual neglect.

terms.definition: The phase in which food is chewed and mixed with saliva to form a cohesive bolus.
terms.exampleSentence: Reduced lingual strength prolonged the oral preparatory phase with solid textures.
terms.contextNotes: Requires intact dentition, lingual movement and labial seal.

terms.definition: The voluntary phase in which the bolus is propelled posteriorly by the tongue toward the pharynx.
terms.exampleSentence: The oral phase was prolonged with anterior loss of bolus due to weak labial seal.
terms.contextNotes: Typically lasts under one second in healthy adults.

terms.definition: The involuntary swallow phase in which the bolus passes through the pharynx with airway protection.
terms.exampleSentence: VFSS demonstrated a delayed pharyngeal phase with reduced laryngeal elevation.
terms.contextNotes: Critical for airway protection; impairment carries the highest aspiration risk.

terms.definition: The involuntary phase during which peristalsis transports the bolus through the oesophagus to the stomach.
terms.exampleSentence: The oesophageal phase was unremarkable on barium swallow with no holdup observed.
terms.contextNotes: Typically outside SLT scope; refer to gastroenterology if concerned.

terms.definition: An acute neurological deficit due to interruption of cerebral blood supply, often causing dysphagia or aphasia.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient was admitted with a left MCA stroke and presented with aphasia and dysphagia.
terms.contextNotes: All acute stroke patients require swallow screening within four hours.

terms.definition: A progressive decline in cognition affecting memory, communication and often eating and swallowing skills.
terms.exampleSentence: In the late stages of dementia, the patient required modified textures to manage feeding safely.
terms.contextNotes: Avoid the term 'demented'; refer to a person living with dementia.

terms.definition: A progressive neurodegenerative disease causing weakness of bulbar and limb muscles, leading to dysarthria and dysphagia.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient with motor neurone disease was referred for AAC and dysphagia management.
terms.contextNotes: Early SLT involvement supports proactive AAC and PEG decisions.

terms.definition: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder causing tremor, rigidity and hypokinetic dysarthria with possible dysphagia.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient with Parkinson's disease was referred for LSVT LOUD to address reduced vocal volume.
terms.contextNotes: LSVT LOUD is the evidence-based intervention for Parkinson's voice.

terms.definition: Acquired brain damage from external force, often causing cognitive-communication and swallowing difficulties.
terms.exampleSentence: Following traumatic brain injury, the patient presented with cognitive-communication impairment.
terms.contextNotes: Severity is often graded by Glasgow Coma Scale and PTA duration.

terms.definition: Acute idiopathic unilateral facial nerve weakness affecting facial movement and oral seal during eating.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient with Bell's palsy reported drooling and difficulty maintaining a labial seal.
terms.contextNotes: Most cases resolve spontaneously within several weeks.

terms.definition: Malignancy affecting the upper aerodigestive tract, frequently impacting voice, speech and swallowing.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient with head and neck cancer was referred for pre-treatment swallowing assessment.
terms.contextNotes: Pre-habilitation swallow exercises improve post-treatment outcomes.

terms.definition: Late-onset swallowing difficulty caused by radiation-induced fibrosis of head and neck structures.
terms.exampleSentence: Five years post-treatment, the patient developed post-radiotherapy dysphagia with stricture.
terms.contextNotes: May present years after treatment; long-term SLT follow-up is recommended.

terms.definition: Paired spaces between the base of the tongue and the epiglottis where bolus residue can collect.
terms.exampleSentence: Vallecular residue was cleared with a second dry swallow on instrumental assessment.
terms.contextNotes: Pooling here suggests reduced base-of-tongue retraction.

terms.definition: Paired recesses lateral to the larynx where residue may collect, indicating reduced pharyngeal clearance.
terms.exampleSentence: Bilateral pyriform sinus residue was observed on FEES, suggesting weak pharyngeal contraction.
terms.contextNotes: Asymmetric residue may indicate unilateral pharyngeal weakness.

terms.definition: Lung infection caused by inhalation of food, fluids or oral secretions into the airway.
terms.exampleSentence: The patient developed aspiration pneumonia following recurrent silent aspiration of fluids.
terms.contextNotes: Oral hygiene is a key modifiable risk factor for aspiration pneumonia.

terms.definition: A real-time X-ray study of swallowing using barium-contrasted boluses to assess all swallow phases.
terms.exampleSentence: Videofluoroscopy confirmed silent aspiration on thin fluids and safe swallow on Level 2 fluids.
terms.contextNotes: Gold-standard instrumental swallow assessment with radiation exposure.

terms.definition: Fibreoptic endoscopic evaluation of swallowing using a transnasal endoscope to view the pharynx and larynx.
terms.exampleSentence: FEES was completed at the bedside, demonstrating penetration but no aspiration on Level 0 fluids.
terms.contextNotes: Bedside option without radiation; cannot visualise the moment of swallow itself.
